title: Lustre compat: wire validate_target into build/package/deploy commands
description:
Hook the compatibility gate into every command that produces or
rebuilds Lustre artifacts, so an attendee who modifies Lustre
source gets the gate fire in seconds, not halfway through a
build.

## Commands to wire up

- cmd_build_all → gate at phase 0, before container build
- cmd_build_kernel → gate at start
- cmd_build_lustre → gate at start (primary value for attendees)
- cmd_package → gate at start (mode is server_*, Lustre must be
  buildable to ship)
- cmd_deploy → gate when --build triggers a rebuild path

Each command:
1. Resolves the lustre tree the same way it does today
2. Calls validate_target(tc, lustre_tree)
3. On "ok": proceed silently
4. On "best_effort": print a single calm warning line, proceed
5. On "refuse": emit the ValidationResult's message, exit with
   EXIT_ERROR unless --force was passed
6. On "error": emit the parse error message, exit EXIT_ERROR

## --force flag

Add --force to all of build-all, build-kernel, build-lustre,
deploy, package (build-lustre already has --force for a different
purpose — confirm it's reusable here or rename one of them).

Per-invocation only.  No persistent state.

## Tests

Integration tests in test_ltvm_cli.py that:
- Stub validate_target to return ok/refuse/best_effort
- Call each command with a stubbed TargetConfig + fake tree
- Assert behavior (proceed / warn / exit_error / --force override)

Child of lustre_test_vms_v2-afr.
Depends on: lustre_test_vms_v2-r4h

title: Lustre compat: validate_target() + ltvm validate command
description:
The compatibility gate itself, plus a read-only CLI command to
run it in isolation.

## validate_target(tc, lustre_tree) -> ValidationResult

Lives in ltvm_pkg/lustre_compat.py (alongside the parsers).

Combines:
- tc.lustre_target (e.g. "5.14-rhel9.7")
- tc.lustre_mode (e.g. "server_ldiskfs")
- parse_target_in(tree, tc.lustre_target) → expected kernel version
- parse_which_patch(tree) → declared series → kernel mapping
- parse_changelog(tree) → declared support lists

Returns a ValidationResult with:
- status: "ok" | "best_effort" | "refuse" | "error"
- mode: the target's declared mode
- kernel_version: the version parse_target_in gave us
- matched_in: "which_patch_primary" | "changelog_primary" |
  "changelog_best_effort" | "not_listed"
- message: human-readable explanation, always present

Decision table:

| mode           | check                                         | status       |
|----------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------|
| server_ldiskfs | which_patch has (series, kver) exact match   | ok           |
| server_ldiskfs | which_patch has series but kver doesn't match| refuse       |
| server_ldiskfs | which_patch doesn't mention series at all    | refuse       |
| server_zfs     | ChangeLog server_primary contains kver        | ok           |
| server_zfs     | ChangeLog server_best_effort contains kver    | best_effort  |
| server_zfs     | neither                                       | refuse       |

best_effort → print a calm one-line warning, proceed.

## ltvm validate <target> --lustre-tree <tree>

New subcommand in cli.py.  Runs validate_target() and prints the
ValidationResult in a useful form.  --json for machine output.

Exit codes:
- 0: ok or best_effort
- 1: refuse
- 2: error (e.g. Lustre tree can't be parsed)

## Tests

tests/test_lustre_compat.py:
- Fixture Lustre tree covering all the decision-table rows
- Each row gets a test
- test for ValidationResult shape
- test for "can't parse ChangeLog" → status=error

tests/test_ltvm_cli.py:
- TestCmdValidate: happy path, refuse exit code, --force override
  exit code override, --json output

Child of lustre_test_vms_v2-afr.
Depends on: lustre_test_vms_v2-qb4 (parsers), lustre_test_vms_v2-laj (mode field).

title: Lustre compat: [lustre] mode field in target config
description:
Add a REQUIRED [lustre] section to targets/<target>/kernel.conf
with one field: mode.

Valid values for initial implementation:
- server_ldiskfs
- server_zfs

client mode is deferred (separate future issue, because client
doesn't even need a kernel build).

No default, no back-compat.  kernel.conf without [lustre] mode
raises a clear validation error at target load time.

## Changes

- ltvm_pkg/target_config.py:
  - Parse [lustre] mode in TargetConfig.__init__
  - Store as tc.lustre_mode
  - Raise ValueError on missing / unknown mode
  - Validate mode against a small enum (LustreMode)

- targets/rocky9/kernel.conf:
  - Add [lustre] mode = server_ldiskfs
- targets/rocky8/kernel.conf: same
- targets/rocky10/kernel.conf: same
- targets/ubuntu2404/kernel.conf: what does this even do?  It's
  currently "server: false" in target.conf, so mode = client? but
  we're not implementing client.  May need to set it to
  server_ldiskfs and accept it's broken for now, or leave
  ubuntu2404 out of the migration until client mode is
  implemented.  Flag for discussion.

- tests/test_config.py:
  - Existing target fixture tests need the new field added
  - New test: missing [lustre] mode → ValueError
  - New test: unknown mode value → ValueError

Child of lustre_test_vms_v2-afr.
Depends on: lustre_test_vms_v2-qb4 (parsers — this issue doesn't
use them but the follow-on wire-up does, so order matters for
review clarity).

title: Lustre compat: parsers for which_patch, ChangeLog, target.in
description:
Pure-function parsers in a new ltvm_pkg/lustre_compat.py.  No CLI
wiring, no side effects.

## Functions

- parse_which_patch(tree: Path) -> dict[str, str]
  Reads lustre/kernel_patches/which_patch and returns the
  "PATCH SERIES FOR SERVER KERNELS" table as
  {series_name: kernel_version_string}.

- parse_changelog(tree: Path) -> ChangeLogEntry
  Reads lustre/ChangeLog and returns the TOP entry's declared
  kernel lists, split into server_primary, server_best_effort,
  client_primary, client_best_effort.  Each is list[str] of
  kernel version strings like "5.14.0-611.13.1.el9".

- parse_target_in(tree: Path, series: str) -> TargetIn
  Reads lustre/kernel_patches/targets/<series>.target.in and
  returns lnxmaj, lnxrel, KERNEL_SRPM, SERIES.  We already parse
  this in kernel_build.py — factor it out here and call it from
  there too.

## Tests

Fixture Lustre trees under tests/fixtures/lustre_compat/ with
small hand-written which_patch, ChangeLog, and target.in files.
Cover:
- well-formed which_patch → parses correctly
- well-formed ChangeLog with primary + best-effort lists
- malformed ChangeLog → raises (caller decides what to do)
- missing file → raises FileNotFoundError with helpful message
- target.in with and without LUSTRE_VERSION/EXTRA_VERSION variants

Child of lustre_test_vms_v2-afr.

title: Lustre/kernel compatibility validation — design parent
description:
Make ltvm refuse (or warn) on Lustre/kernel/mode combinations that
Lustre upstream does not declare supported, before spending time
building artifacts.

## Design

- Each target declares a REQUIRED [lustre] mode in kernel.conf.
  Values: server_ldiskfs, server_zfs.  (client deferred — doesn't
  need a kernel build anyway; separate future work.)

- Validation reads two files from the user's --lustre-tree:
  1. lustre/kernel_patches/which_patch — authoritative table mapping
     series name → exact kernel version for server+ldiskfs.
  2. lustre/ChangeLog (top entry) — "Server primary kernels" and
     "Other server kernels known to build and work" lists.

- New ltvm validate command runs ONLY the static check, no builds.

- Validation hooks into every Lustre-producing/consuming command
  so an attendee rebuilding Lustre after a source edit sees the
  failure in seconds, not after a rebuild:
    - build-all
    - build-kernel
    - build-lustre
    - deploy (when --build triggers a rebuild)
    - package

- --force overrides any refusal.  This is the "take over the world"
  escape hatch — we want developers to be able to try combinations
  Lustre hasn't yet declared.  Per-invocation only, no persistence.

- Mode refusal text for best-effort kernels: calm warning, no
  refuse.  The user can read.

- Kernel patches in lustre/kernel_patches/series/<series> are
  applied only for server_ldiskfs mode (we already do this).  For
  server_zfs they're optional performance fixes; skipping them is
  fine.

## Out of scope

- Dynamic canary compile after modules_prepare.  Too complex for
  the benefit; static check is sufficient.
- Upstream Lustre bugs (source declaring APIs that don't exist
  in the target kernel).  We're a build tool, not a Lustre fixer.
- client mode.  Defer entirely — clients don't need a kernel
  build, different flow, different issue.
- Backwards compatibility with existing kernel.conf files.
  Tool is not deployed, we can break the format freely.

## Split into child issues

1. Parsers (which_patch + ChangeLog + target.in)
2. [lustre] mode field in target config
3. validate_target() + ltvm validate command
4. Wire validation into build-all, build-kernel, build-lustre,
   deploy, package
5. --force plumbing through all of the above

title: Unblock workshop build: find a working Lustre+rocky9 combination
description:
Lustre master HEAD (commit 103a214985) does not build against the
5.14-rhel9.7 target — fails compiling ldiskfs with:

  ldiskfs.h:2276: error: 'struct shash_desc' has no member named 'flags'
  osd_internal.h:1629: error: 'ldiskfs_journal_get_write_access' undeclared
  osd_io.c:1258: error: 'LDISKFS_FIEMAP_FLAG_MEMCPY' undeclared

The .target.in and which_patch both declare 611.13.1.el9_7 as the
canonical kernel for 5.14-rhel9.7, so Lustre upstream CLAIMS this
combination is supported.  It just isn't right now.

## Options to unblock

1. Find a Lustre commit where 5.14-rhel9.7 actually builds (git
   log the target.in / ldiskfs series / osd-ldiskfs paths since
   the last working build)
2. Switch workshop target to 5.14-rhel9.6 or 9.5 (stable longer)
3. Switch workshop to rocky8 (rhel8.10 target, rock solid)

Acceptance: `ltvm build-all rocky9 --lustre-tree <tree>` completes
successfully on rockybuild, produces a package that deploys into a
microvm on host1 and runs lustre.

Related: lustre_test_vms_v2-afr (the compat gate is what will
prevent this class of foot-gun in the future).

title: Make cross-compilation a first-class build target
description:
Make cross-compilation (not emulation) explicit and supported for both kernel and Lustre builds, so any host can produce artifacts for any target arch at native toolchain speed.

## Scope

- Build container gains a cross toolchain for the *other* arch:
  - gcc-x86_64-linux-gnu on aarch64 host → x86_64 targets
  - gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu on x86_64 host → aarch64 targets
- Build container runs natively on the host (no --platform linux/amd64, no qemu-user)
- kernel-build-inner.sh invokes 'make ARCH=$target_arch CROSS_COMPILE=$prefix-' when host_arch != target_arch
- Lustre build passes ARCH / CROSS_COMPILE through to autotools + kbuild when crossing
- SRPM build: use rpmbuild --target=$target_arch + a cross-spec tweak, or unpack SRPM and build out-of-tree
- image builds: VM root filesystem for target arch — this IS where we still need some emulation (installing target-arch packages into a target-arch rootfs requires running the target package manager); investigate whether binfmt+qemu-user is needed just for this step, or if we can use a pre-built base image

## Why this matters

- Apple Silicon Macs are ARM64; developers will want to build both ARM64 (native) and x86_64 (cross) from the same box
- Linux x86 CI will want to produce ARM64 artifacts without needing an ARM64 runner
- Emulation (qemu-user in the kernel build path) is ~10x slower; cross-compile is ~native speed

## Non-goals

- qemu-user / binfmt-based foreign-arch builds (rejected — too slow for kernel/Lustre builds)

## Likely file impact

- ltvm_pkg/target_config.py — add target arch as a first-class field
- ltvm_pkg/kernel_build.py + kernel-build-inner.sh — ARCH / CROSS_COMPILE handling
- ltvm_pkg/lustre_build.py — thread cross vars through
- ltvm_pkg/image_build.py — decide how rootfs assembly handles target arch
- targets/*/container.Dockerfile — install cross toolchain

title: macOS host support: handle sudo/user split in ltvm install
description:
On macOS, brew install refuses root but /usr/local/bin symlink + /etc/hosts edits need root. Unlike Linux, we can't just 'sudo ./ltvm install'.

Options:
1. Run entire install as user, invoke 'sudo' selectively for privileged bits (symlink, /etc/hosts, socket_vmnet launchd)
2. Run brew bits as user, then re-exec with sudo for system bits
3. Document a two-phase install

Lima solves this with a sudo helper installed once. Worth mirroring.

Current state: _run_setup_macos() blindly creates /usr/local/bin/ltvm symlink, which will fail without root.

title: macOS host support: gate fcbr0/dnsmasq/TAP paths behind platform check
description:
Existing code in host_setup.py, vm_net.py, qemu_run.py unconditionally creates fcbr0 bridge, TAP devices via 'ip tuntap add', and runs dnsmasq. These paths must be gated so they run only on Linux.

The macOS path (socket_vmnet + /etc/hosts) replaces them entirely — no bridge, no TAP, no dnsmasq on Darwin.

Refactor toward a small net-backend abstraction if clean; otherwise just platform if/else at the call sites.

title: macOS host support: DNS via /etc/hosts (no dnsmasq)
description:
On macOS, skip dnsmasq entirely. Write VM hostname → IP entries to /etc/hosts directly (requires sudo).

Benefits: no SIGHUP reloading, no dnsmasq config generation, no daemon to manage. macOS resolves /etc/hosts natively.

Needs: sudo-gated add/remove of ltvm-managed entries, marker comments to identify ltvm-owned entries for cleanup on VM destroy.

title: macOS host support: socket_vmnet-based VM networking
description:
Implement socket_vmnet networking path in vm_net.py / qemu_run.py for macOS hosts.

Design:
- QEMU connects to socket_vmnet via -netdev stream (Unix socket)
- ltvm allocates static IPs *below* the DHCP range (e.g. 192.168.105.20-.50), guests use static IP via kernel cmdline fc_ip/fc_gw/fc_name (same mechanism as Linux)
- Deterministic MAC per VM (already implemented for Linux — reuse)
- All VMs connect to same socket_vmnet instance → inter-VM traffic works
- Outbound NAT handled by vmnet framework

Risk to validate early: vmnet.framework may drop packets for IPs outside its DHCP table. If so, need to investigate workarounds (use bootpd, or configure vmnet with no DHCP).

Depends on Darwin platform detection + socket_vmnet install being in place.

title: macOS host support: install socket_vmnet via brew
description:
Add socket_vmnet installation to ltvm install flow on macOS. socket_vmnet (from Lima project) runs as a privileged daemon and provides vmnet-shared networking to unprivileged QEMU processes.

brew install socket_vmnet — verify it's present, install if missing with sudo confirmation. Install location typically /opt/homebrew/opt/socket_vmnet/bin/socket_vmnet.

Reference: https://github.com/lima-vm/socket_vmnet

title: macOS host support: hvf accelerator in qemu_machine_for_arch()
description:
ltvm_pkg/vm_state.py qemu_machine_for_arch() currently picks accel=kvm when host arch matches guest, else accel=tcg. On macOS, use accel=hvf for native arch (ARM64 guest on ARM64 Mac), tcg otherwise.

Small, self-contained change. Machine type (virt) already works for ARM64.

title: macOS host support: skip Linux-only tool checks (grub, iptables, /dev/kvm)
description:
host_setup.py checks for grub-install/grub2-install, iptables, parted, /dev/kvm — all Linux-only. On macOS these are either irrelevant (grub, parted — image building isn't supported on Mac) or have different equivalents (iptables → pfctl, but not needed if we use vmnet).

Gate these checks behind platform detection. On macOS, skip them and note image/kernel builds require a Linux host or podman machine.
close_reason: Addressed by merge 904faa1: is_macos() helper added; run_setup() early-returns on macOS via _run_setup_macos(); verify() branches on platform. HostInfo is only constructed inside run_setup(), so Darwin path never hits Linux-only probes.

title: macOS host support: replace /proc reads with sysctl/ps equivalents
description:
Replace Linux-specific /proc reads with macOS equivalents, gated on platform detection:
- /proc/meminfo → sysctl hw.memsize (qemu_run.py:41, vm_commands.py host memory check)
- /proc/<pid>/comm → ps -p <pid> -o comm= (qemu_run.py:143 for VM process validation)
- /proc/version, /proc/cmdline — skip WSL2/nested-virt detection on macOS

Keep Linux paths intact; branch on platform.

title: macOS host support: Darwin platform detection in HostInfo
description:
Add Darwin branch to ltvm_pkg/host_setup.py HostInfo class. Currently reads /etc/os-release and detects dnf/apt — need to handle macOS (platform.system()=='Darwin') and populate fields from sysctl/system_profiler where appropriate.

Acceptance: ltvm running on macOS no longer crashes at HostInfo init; identifies the host as macOS with version info.

Foundation issue — many other macOS tasks depend on this platform detection existing.
close_reason: Addressed by merge 904faa1: is_macos() helper added; run_setup() early-returns on macOS via _run_setup_macos(); verify() branches on platform. HostInfo is only constructed inside run_setup(), so Darwin path never hits Linux-only probes.

title: macOS host support: podman machine setup guidance + preflight
description:
Container builds (kernel, image, lustre) work on Mac via 'podman machine'. One-time setup:
  podman machine init
  podman machine start

ltvm should:
1. On macOS, in build commands, detect if podman machine is running ('podman machine list --format json')
2. If not running, print a clear message: 'podman machine not running; run: podman machine start'
3. If not initialized, print init instructions

Not a blocker for VM running (doesn't need podman) — only for build commands.

Also document this in CLAUDE.md macOS section.

title: Publish ARM64 target artifacts (kernel + VM image) to GitHub releases
description:
Mac users running on Apple Silicon need ARM64 pre-built artifacts to skip from-source builds. Currently release_package.py only publishes the x86 target.

Work:
- Set up an ARM64 build target (likely rocky9-arm64 or similar)
- Publish ARM64 kernel + VM image to GitHub releases alongside x86
- 'ltvm fetch rocky9' should auto-select arch based on host (or we name targets with arch)

Blocker: need the ARM64 target working end-to-end on a Linux ARM64 host first (CI or local).

title: macOS host support: documentation and unsupported-operation messages
description:
Document macOS support in CLAUDE.md / README:
- Supported: ARM64 VMs on Apple Silicon via hvf + socket_vmnet
- Supported (via podman machine): kernel + image + Lustre builds (native ARM64 speed)
- Unsupported on Mac: x86 guests (would need qemu-user cross-compile), VFIO
- For unsupported operations: ltvm should exit with a clear message pointing to a Linux host
- Setup prerequisites: brew, qemu, socket_vmnet, podman + podman machine

Also update SUGGESTED-AGENTS.md if needed.

title: macOS host support: socket_vmnet lifecycle (launchd or on-demand)
description:
socket_vmnet needs to be running before any VM starts. Two options:

1. launchd plist installed by 'ltvm install' — always-on daemon
2. Start-on-demand: ltvm starts socket_vmnet when first VM boots, leaves it running

Lima itself uses option 2. Lower setup friction, no persistent daemon when not in use.

Implement whichever fits ltvm's model better. Include stop/cleanup on 'ltvm destroy' when no VMs remain (optional).

title: Future: support building Lustre against a vanilla (unpatched) kernel
description:
Not a TODO for right now — placeholder for a capability we will
want eventually.

## The use case

Lustre supports building against vanilla kernels (see lustre/ChangeLog
under 'Other server kernels known to build and work at some point'):
  - vanilla linux 5.4.0 / 5.4.21 / 5.4.136 (ZFS + ldiskfs)
  - vanilla linux 6.1.36 / 6.6.13 (ZFS + ldiskfs)

Client builds ALSO work against any unpatched kernel since 2.6.18
(stated explicitly in lustre/kernel_patches/which_patch).

A developer or tester might want to:
1. Bring their own kernel tree (git clone linux; make; etc.), not
   a RHEL/SLES SRPM
2. Build Lustre (client, server+zfs, or even server+ldiskfs without
   the performance patches) against it
3. Boot a microvm with that kernel and run tests

Today ltvm build-kernel requires a RHEL/SLES SRPM as input (see
kernel_build.py) — it's hardcoded to extract and patch an SRPM.
There's no path to "use this kernel build-tree I already have" or
"clone and build this kernel repo."

## Scope sketch (for whenever we pick this up)

Probably a new mode and a new target type:

- Target config grows a "kernel source" option distinct from
  "kernel SRPM + patch series":
    [kernel]
    source = srpm        # today
    OR
    source = git         # clone a repo at a ref
    OR
    source = tree        # user-provided tree path

- Target mode gains vanilla variants:
    [lustre]
    mode = client_vanilla
    mode = server_zfs_vanilla
    mode = server_ldiskfs_vanilla  # ldiskfs patches still apply,
                                   # kernel patches skipped

- Validation against the vanilla kernel uses ChangeLog's
  "Other kernels known to build" lists rather than which_patch,
  since vanilla kernels aren't in which_patch.

- build-kernel needs a new code path that doesn't go through the
  SRPM extract step.

- The canary/validation logic extends naturally — we still probe
  "does this kernel build-tree work" the same way.

## Why this is deferred

The RHEL/SLES-targeted flow is what we need for the workshop and
for server builds.  Vanilla kernel support is a developer ergonomics
win, not a correctness gate.  Will be easier to add once the
[lustre] mode + validate_target machinery from
lustre_test_vms_v2-afr is in place.

Related: lustre_test_vms_v2-afr (foundation), and the client mode
deferral called out in lustre_test_vms_v2-laj.

value: Lustre build flow has THREE patch hierarchies to understand: (1) lustre/kernel_patches/series/<target>.series — applied to the kernel SRPM before building the kernel; these are performance fixes, optional for correctness but recommended. (2) ldiskfs/kernel_patches/series/ldiskfs-<target>.series — applied INSIDE the Lustre build to ext4 source copied out of the kernel, producing ldiskfs; these are REQUIRED for server_ldiskfs mode and Lustre applies them itself, we should NOT touch them. (3) Lustre source itself, compiled against the patched kernel build-tree. The authoritative target<->kernel version mapping for server_ldiskfs lives in lustre/kernel_patches/which_patch. ChangeLog top entry lists server_primary + server_best_effort + client_primary + client_best_effort kernel versions for the current development release. which_patch explicitly states: client needs no kernel patches since 2.6.18, server+ZFS needs no kernel patches, only server+ldiskfs needs them (and the ldiskfs patches, which Lustre handles internally).
